plain: How likely the model thinks heart or blood-vessel disease may be present, shown as a percentage. Not a doctor's diagnosis.
Your age in years. Older age can increase heart-disease risk in population studies.
Biological sex coded as 0 (female) or 1 (male) in this dataset. Used because risk patterns differ between groups in the research data.
What kind of chest discomfort you have: 0 = typical angina (often with exertion), 1 = atypical, 2 = non-anginal, 3 = no chest pain symptoms.
The top number in a blood pressure reading (pressure when the heart beats). Example: 120 in "120/80."
A blood fat measured in mg/dL. Very high values are linked to artery plaque over time.
Whether blood sugar was over 120 mg/dL after fasting: 0 = no, 1 = yes. High values can relate to diabetes risk.
A heart tracing at rest: 0 = normal, 1 = ST-T wave abnormality, 2 = possible left ventricular hypertrophy (thickened heart muscle).
The highest heart rate reached during an exercise stress test. Lower than expected for age can sometimes signal heart strain.
Chest pain triggered by exercise: 0 = no, 1 = yes.
How much the ST segment dips on an exercise ECG compared with rest. Higher numbers can mean more stress-related changes.
Shape of the ST segment during peak exercise: 0 = upsloping, 1 = flat, 2 = downsloping (often considered more concerning).
Number of major heart arteries (0–3) that looked narrowed on an imaging test called fluoroscopy.
Blood-flow pattern on a nuclear scan: 1 = normal, 2 = fixed defect, 3 = reversible defect (reversible can mean reduced flow under stress).
Chest pain or pressure, often from reduced blood flow to the heart muscle.
A computer method that learns patterns from many past patient records to estimate risk on new inputs.
